text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Donald Trump', 'Trump administration', 'Federal Reserve', 'U.S. Trade Representative', 'Treasury Secretary'], topics=['economic policy', 'fiscal stimulus', 'tax cuts', 'infrastructure spending', 'interest rates', 'trade policy', 'tariffs', 'recession risk'].

## SUPPORTS-YES (12)
- [2025-04-02] President Donald Trump hosted a "Liberation Day" event in the White House Rose Garden on Wednesday, April 2, 2025, to announce new tariffs and sign an executive order implementing them.

The new policy includes a minimum 10% tariff on all imports, with specific rates such as 34% on China, 20% on the European Union, 46% on Vietnam, 32% on Taiwan, 49% on Cambodia, and 44% on Sri Lanka. The baseline 10% tariff is scheduled to take effect on April 5, with other tariffs commencing April 9.

Trump also signed an order removing a "de minimis" tariff exemption on low-cost products and proposed an "External Revenue Service" to collect tariffs. The event featured auto workers Brian Pannebecker and Chris Vitale, who voiced support for the tariffs.

Following the announcement, made after U.S. stock markets closed, stock index futures declined. New Zealand's Trade Minister confirmed the impending 10% tariff on its exports but stated no plans for retaliatory measures.
- [2025-04-02] President Donald J. Trump declared a national emergency and signed an Executive Order on April 2, 2025, citing U.S. goods trade deficits. The order identified a lack of reciprocity in bilateral trade relationships, disparate tariff rates, non-tariff barriers, and U.S. trading partners’ economic policies as a threat to national security and the economy. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA), the order directs the imposition of responsive tariffs. A 10% tariff will apply to all countries effective April 5, 2025. Individualized higher tariffs will be imposed on countries with the largest trade deficits with the U.S., effective April 9, 2025. The order states these tariffs will remain until the threat is resolved. Exemptions include specific steel/aluminum articles, autos/auto parts, copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, bullion, and certain energy/minerals. The action follows investigations directed by presidential memorandums in January and February 2025.
- [2025-04-02] On April 2, 2025, President Donald Trump signed Executive Order 14257, titled "Regulating Imports With a Reciprocal Tariff To Rectify Trade Practices That Contribute to Large and Persistent Annual United States Goods Trade Deficits." The order declared a national emergency over the U.S. trade deficit, citing it as an unusual and extraordinary threat to national security and the economy due to factors including a lack of reciprocity and disparate tariff rates. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act, the order authorized tariffs. A baseline 10% tariff for nearly all countries took effect April 5, with higher country-specific rates for major trading partners scheduled for April 9. Trump also signed Executive Order 14256, closing the de minimis exemption for imports from China. The announcement, which Trump designated "Liberation Day," contributed to the 2025 stock market crash. The U.S. Court of International Trade later ruled on May 28, 2025, that Trump had overstepped his authority under IEEPA, ordering the tariffs vacated.
- [2025-04-06] President Trump announced a new global tariff regime at a press conference on April 6, 2025.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent discussed the policy, stating it aimed to reindustrialize the U.S. and address national and economic security concerns. Bessent described the policy as "transformational for the American economy and workers."

Bessent projected the new tariffs could generate $300 billion to $600 billion annually, which he said could help fund government initiatives and potentially reduce the tax burden on the middle class. He stated the long-term goal of the tariffs is to incentivize manufacturers to move factories to the U.S., which he said would lead to a decrease in tariffs over time as domestic production increases, and revenue shifts to income taxes from new jobs.

The policy is being implemented through executive authority.
- [2025-04-02] President Donald Trump announced and signed executive orders for new tariffs on Wednesday, April 2, 2025, during an event in the Rose Garden at the White House. The measures include a 10% baseline tax on imports from all countries, with higher rates imposed on dozens of nations running trade surpluses with the United States. Specific tariffs include a 34% tax on imports from China, 20% on the European Union, 24% on Japan, and 32% on Taiwan. Trump stated the tariffs are intended to boost U.S. manufacturing, generate revenue, and address global trade practices. He declared a national economic emergency to implement these tariffs under the 1977 International Emergency Powers Act. The 10% baseline rate is set to begin collection on Saturday, April 5th, with higher rates starting April 9th. Exemptions for imports from China worth $800 or less were also removed.
- [2025-04-02] President Donald J. Trump declared a national emergency and signed an Executive Order on April 2, 2025, citing U.S. goods trade deficits. The order identified a lack of reciprocity in bilateral trade relationships, disparate tariff rates, non-tariff barriers, and U.S. trading partners’ economic policies as a threat to national security and the economy. Individualized higher tariffs will be imposed on countries with the largest trade deficits with the U.S., effective April 9, 2025. The order states these tariffs will remain until the threat is resolved. Exemptions include specific steel/aluminum articles, autos/auto parts, copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, bullion, and certain energy/minerals. The action follows investigations directed by presidential memorandums in January and February 2025.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent discussed the policy, stating it aimed to reindustrialize the U.S. and address national and economic security concerns. Bessent described the policy as "transformational for the American economy and workers."

- [2025-04-02] President Donald J. Trump declared a national emergency on April 2, 2025, citing foreign trade and economic practices that have created large and persistent U.S. goods trade deficits. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA), President Trump ordered the imposition of tariffs. A 10% tariff will apply to all countries, effective April 5, 2025, at 12:01 a.m. EDT. Subsequently, individualized higher tariffs will be imposed on countries with the largest trade deficits, taking effect April 9, 2025, at 12:01 a.m. EDT. These tariffs will remain in effect until the underlying nonreciprocal treatment and trade deficit threat are resolved. The order also grants modification authority to adjust tariffs based on trading partners' actions and specifies exemptions for certain goods, while maintaining existing IEEPA orders for Canada and Mexico.

Following the announcement, made after U.S. stock markets closed, stock index futures declined. New Zealand's Trade Minister confirmed the impending 10% tariff on its exports but stated no plans for retaliatory measures.
